original_abstract:
Electrocatalysis offers a transformative pathway for the local, on-demand production of industrial chemicals using renewable energy, bolstering grid resilience and streamlining supply logistics. In this study, we demonstrate the efficacy of molecular copper boron-imidazolate cages, BIF-29(Cu), in facilitating the electrocatalytic coupling of CO₂ and NO₃⁻ to synthesize urea. Despite featuring isolated single-metal sites, BIF-29(Cu) emerges as one of the most selective systems reported to date for this complex C-N coupling, achieving a Faradaic efficiency of 68.5% and a partial current density of 424 μA cm⁻².

To unravel the mechanism behind C-N bond formation, we employed a multipronged approach integrating electrochemical analysis, in situ surface-enhanced infrared absorption spectroscopy (SEIRAS), X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS), and density functional theory (DFT) simulations. Our findings reveal that NO₃⁻ and CO₂ reduction occur in tandem at distinct copper sites within the supramolecular framework. The most energetically favorable pathway involves a cascade mechanism where *CO and NH₂OH intermediates condense to form urea. This work underscores the potential of supramolecular metal-organic cages with well-defined, discrete active sites to drive highly efficient and sophisticated coupling reactions.
humanized_abstract:
Electrocatalysis can be an energy-efficient method for the local and on-demand production of bulk chemicals using renewable energy, thereby enhancing the stability of the power grid and optimizing logistics. Here, we show that molecular copper boron-imidazolate frameworks (BIFs), namely molecular BIF-29(Cu), can catalyze the electrochemical reaction of CO2 with NO3− to produce urea. Notably, in spite of consisting of only isolated single-metal sites, molecular BIF-29(Cu) emerges as one of the most selective molecular catalysts reported for this multi-step C-N bond-forming transformation while yielding a Faradaic efficiency of 68.5% and a partial current density of 424 μA cm−2.

To gain insight into the mechanism of the C-N bond formation event, a synergistic approach was used which combined electrochemical techniques with in situ surface enhanced infrared absorption spectroscopy (SEIRAS), X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) and density functional theory (DFT) calculations. It was revealed that NO₃⁻ and CO₂ can be reduced through different copper sites within the host architecture, and the reaction channel with the lowest energy barrier is a cascade process in which *CO and NH₂OH react to give urea. In this communication, a discrete supramolecular metal-organic cage with well-defined active sites was designed and showed high catalytic activity for complex coupling reactions such as a highly efficient synergetic catalytic reduction of nitrates and carbon dioxide to produce valuable products at a low overpotential.
